consulting-engineer-professional-service.whatThisIs: Consulting Engineer / Professional Services Engineer is billable, project-scoped, client-embedded technical delivery — implementing and customizing a product (or a multi-vendor stack) against a specific client's environment under a statement of work with a defined end date. It's not glorified IT helpdesk: postings consistently require architecture-level input (guiding design decisions, not just following them), custom integration and scripting work, and technical ownership through go-live. It's also distinct from a Forward Deployed Engineer, who ships production code that merges back into the core product with no utilization target — a Consulting Engineer's defining number is billable hours against a scoped engagement, not a merged PR.
consulting-engineer-professional-service.whyMatchedTemplate:
You matched with Consulting Engineer / Professional Services Engineer because your answers put {{top_dimension_1}}, {{top_dimension_2}}, and {{top_dimension_3}} close to what this role actually demands day to day — those are the parts of your profile pulling hardest toward this match.

For example: a profile with high **Technical Breadth vs. Depth**, high **Stakeholder & Client-Facing Comfort**, and moderate **Variable Compensation Appetite** would land here — someone comfortable ranging across platforms, integration technologies, and cloud services as a given engagement demands, who treats client workshops and architecture reviews as a sustained daily mode rather than an occasional task, and who's fine with a real utilization-linked bonus riding alongside base pay without needing full deal-by-deal commission risk.
consulting-engineer-professional-service.aDayInThisRole: Your week is organized around a scoped SOW: technical discovery and architecture-review workshops with client stakeholders, hands-on configuration and integration work (APIs, data migration, custom scripting — SQL shows up constantly as a baseline skill), and running a structured delivery program against a timeline with named milestones. You're watching your own utilization number — the target band is commonly 75-85% — alongside the engagement's schedule and cost performance, because scope creep on a fixed-price SOW erodes margin fast and that's tracked, not incidental. Travel is bimodal: pure consultancies historically ran 4-5 days/week on client sites (shifting post-COVID toward every-other-week hybrid), while vendor in-house PS roles run lighter, client-site time mostly at kickoffs and key milestones. Documentation — implementation standards, client-facing runbooks — is a deliverable you own, not an afterthought.
consulting-engineer-professional-service.compStructure: Base plus variable, with the variable piece tied to billable utilization and project margin — not to closed sales quota the way Sales Engineering is. A commonly cited modeled split is roughly 70% base / 30% incentive (e.g., a consultant billing 80% utilization at a $200/hr rate generates enough billed revenue that a 10% bonus lands near that 30% mark). Vendor in-house PS comp leans closer to standard base+bonus+equity tech comp and prioritizes utilization less heavily than pure consultancies do; entry/mid consultancy base pay (Deloitte, Accenture) commonly runs $80K-$110K, with utilization-linked bonus layered on top and rising at senior/principal levels.
consulting-engineer-professional-service.growthAreasTemplate: If {{growth_dimension}} came back lower than this role typically wants, that's worth a closer look rather than a red flag — it usually means a specific, testable gap, not a wrong match. For example: if your **Variable Compensation Appetite** score is on the low side, know that utilization is treated as a real, actively-managed KPI here (not a background metric) — firms explicitly track it above 80% as a burnout risk in the other direction, but a modest bonus genuinely rides on your billable-hours percentage, so if you want compensation fully decoupled from any hour-tracked target, that structural fact is worth weighing regardless of how strong your delivery skills are.
consulting-engineer-professional-service.howToTestCheaply: Track your own hours against a self-imposed "billable vs. non-billable" split for a few weeks on your current job — including the unglamorous admin and internal-meeting time — and see how it feels to actively manage that ratio rather than ignore it. Separately, ask a consulting or vendor-PS engineer to walk you through how a recent SOW's scope crept and how they handled the client conversation about it — that negotiation, done under a live budget and timeline, is the part of the job a portfolio project can't simulate.

customer-support-engineer.whatThisIs: Customer Support Engineer is reactive, ticket-and-SLA-driven technical support at a software company — not tier-1 script-reading, and not the same job as a Customer Success/Solutions Engineer who owns a named account book. The modern version of this role means reading distributed traces, writing SQL, testing a cURL command against a misconfigured webhook, and shipping a fix within the first response — real production-tooling debugging, done against an inbound queue rather than a project plan. Work arrives ticket by ticket from a shared queue, not from one persistent relationship, and success is measured against explicit numeric targets (SLA, CSAT, FCR), not a qualitative "how's the relationship going."
customer-support-engineer.whyMatchedTemplate:
You matched with Customer Support Engineer because your answers put {{top_dimension_1}}, {{top_dimension_2}}, and {{top_dimension_3}} close to what this role actually demands day to day — those are the parts of your profile pulling hardest toward this match.

For example: a profile with high **Interrupt Tolerance**, high **Debugging / Diagnostic Depth**, and high **On-Call / Incident Appetite** would land here — someone energized by a stream of unplanned, arriving-on-someone-else's-timeline tickets rather than experiencing them as a tax on real work, who can diagnose across app code, infra, and third-party behavior fast, and who doesn't mind occasional weekend on-call windows for the highest-severity cases.
customer-support-engineer.aDayInThisRole: Your day is organized around a ticket queue, not a calendar of self-scheduled blocks: you triage and reproduce customer-reported issues, often with direct system access rather than screenshots and guesswork. A typical resolution might mean reading a distributed trace, writing a SQL query to check what the data actually says, or flipping a feature flag to resolve something without looping in engineering. You'll track your own performance against first-response-time and time-to-resolution targets tiered by severity, and you'll partner with Engineering, Product, and Customer Success on escalations that need more than a queue-level fix. At the more senior end (or at the SRE-adjacent boundary, like Cloudflare's Customer Reliability Engineer track), that shifts toward owning the most complex, highest-severity incidents end-to-end, including weekend on-call.
customer-support-engineer.compStructure: Base plus equity, with variable comp structured as a modest bonus rather than commission — this is IC-engineering comp, not sales comp. Reported bands run roughly $100K-$160K base at startups/scaleups (Vercel, PagerDuty), with Big Tech levels running higher (Amazon Support/Cloud Support Engineer roughly $123K-$243K total comp across L4-L5; Microsoft $100K-$235K+ depending on level). One posting's mention of "commission" for a support IC role is flagged in the research as likely generic benefits boilerplate, not a real commission plan — don't expect deal-linked upside here.
customer-support-engineer.growthAreasTemplate: If {{growth_dimension}} came back lower than this role typically wants, that's worth a closer look rather than a red flag — it usually means a specific, testable gap, not a wrong match. For example: if your **Interrupt Tolerance** score is on the low side, know that this role's defining trait is that work arrives primarily via an inbound queue on someone else's clock, all day — if you want long, self-scheduled blocks of uninterrupted work, a queue-driven job like this one will likely feel like constant friction rather than a rewarding challenge, however good you are at the debugging itself.
customer-support-engineer.howToTestCheaply: Spend a day answering technical questions on a public forum (a product's Discord/Discourse, or Stack Overflow tagged for a tool you use) under a self-imposed response-time target, and notice whether the "someone else's clock, right now" pressure feels energizing or draining. Separately, ask a Support Engineer at a company you admire for 20 minutes to walk you through a real recent ticket end-to-end — the debugging tools they reached for tell you more about the actual job than the title does.

customer-support-solutions-engineer.whatThisIs: Customer Success / Customer Solutions Engineer is the proactive, named-account-embedded technical advisor role — the deliberate opposite pole from Customer Support Engineer, not a friendlier label for the same job. Where Support works an inbound ticket queue against SLA targets, a CSE owns a book of accounts through onboarding, adoption, and ongoing technical health, running workshops and building enablement plans rather than triaging tickets. It still requires real product depth (every posting reviewed requires hands-on fluency sufficient to run technical workshops and resolve non-trivial implementation issues) — the difference from Support is *when* that depth gets applied: proactive design work on a scheduled cadence, not reactive debugging against a clock.
customer-support-solutions-engineer.whyMatchedTemplate:
You matched with Customer Success / Customer Solutions Engineer because your answers put {{top_dimension_1}}, {{top_dimension_2}}, and {{top_dimension_3}} close to what this role actually demands day to day — those are the parts of your profile pulling hardest toward this match.

For example: a profile with high **Relationship Continuity**, high **Ambiguity Tolerance**, and high **Teaching / Explaining Enjoyment** would land here — someone who wants a persistent, named relationship with the same accounts over time rather than an anonymous queue, who's comfortable being judged on a qualitative "trusted-advisor" signal instead of a crisp SLA number, and who enjoys designing and running enablement workshops as a core part of the job, not a side task.
customer-support-solutions-engineer.aDayInThisRole: Your day runs on a scheduled cadence — recurring account check-ins, quarterly business reviews alongside a Customer Success Manager, and project milestones — not an inbound queue or on-call rotation. You might spend a morning designing a custom adoption plan for one account, an afternoon running a technical workshop to unblock a stalled integration, and log usage/health signals to flag risk to your CSM partner before it becomes a renewal conversation. At companies like Databricks, CSEs act as "quarterbacks" — curating recommendations and coordinating specialist Solutions Architects for the deepest technical work rather than doing all of it themselves. You'll get pulled into a genuinely hard technical issue for one of your accounts sometimes, but that's the exception, not the operating mode.
customer-support-solutions-engineer.compStructure: Base-heavy, with a modest annual bonus — not sales-style commission, and (unlike a CSM) you're rarely comped directly on renewal or expansion even though your technical health signal is a real leading indicator partner teams rely on. Direct CSE-labeled comp data is thinner than for Support Engineer roles; the closest corroborating bands (Datadog's broader "Customer Success" band at roughly $79K-$113K total comp, and general market positioning "competitive with mid-level SWE at the same company") suggest a structure similar to Support Engineering's base+bonus pattern, just without a clean multi-level published band of its own.
customer-support-solutions-engineer.growthAreasTemplate: If {{growth_dimension}} came back lower than this role typically wants, that's worth a closer look rather than a red flag — it usually means a specific, testable gap, not a wrong match. For example: if your **Relationship Continuity** score is on the low side, know that the organizing unit of this job is a persistent, named account relationship, not a ticket queue or a rotating portfolio — if you'd rather have variety across many anonymous, interchangeable problems than depth with the same handful of customers over months or years, that's the sharpest mismatch to weigh here.
customer-support-solutions-engineer.howToTestCheaply: Volunteer to run one customer-facing onboarding or enablement session at your current company (even informally, for an internal "customer" like another team adopting your service) and see whether designing the workshop and following up over weeks feels satisfying or like unwanted relationship-management overhead. Separately, shadow a CSE or CSM for a quarterly business review — the qualitative "how's this account really doing" conversation, with no scorecard to hide behind, is the clearest test of whether ambiguous success criteria energize or frustrate you.

data-engineer.whatThisIs: A Data Engineer designs, builds, and operates the pipelines that move data from source systems into forms other people and systems — analysts, data scientists, downstream products — can reliably use. The center of gravity is data movement, transformation, and platform reliability, not modeling or analysis on top of the data. It's commonly confused with "data scientist but more technical" — reality: these are different jobs with different outputs, and the titles get used inconsistently enough across companies that the confusion is understandable, not a sign you're missing something obvious.
data-engineer.whyMatchedTemplate: Your answers point here because you scored high on {{top_dimension_1}}, {{top_dimension_2}}, and {{top_dimension_3}} — these map closely to what actually separates a good data engineer from someone who just knows SQL. A real profile landing here often scores high on Systems Design at Scale, On-Call / Incident Appetite, and Debugging / Diagnostic Depth — you're comfortable architecting a warehouse or streaming platform meant to hold up as the company scales, you can tolerate being paged when a pipeline breaks overnight, and you can chase a root cause across schema, cluster, and job-scheduling layers without a clean single-layer stack trace to follow.
data-engineer.aDayInThisRole: Expect to build and maintain ETL/ELT pipelines (batch and streaming), design warehouse/lakehouse schemas for cost and query performance, and implement data-quality checks and freshness monitors as first-class engineering work, not an afterthought. A first-person practitioner account describes a task that "should take a day" — adding one new event stream — actually taking three weeks once schema discovery, Spark/JVM debugging, and cluster-contention troubleshooting were included, with time split roughly evenly across discovery, development, debugging, and production operations. Many data engineers check a monitoring dashboard before Slack each morning, since pipeline failures often surface overnight, and afternoons skew meeting-heavy (analytics syncs, PM planning, schema discussions) rather than pure build time. A widely cited (if single-source) survey found 97% of data engineers report burnout, with manual firefighting and being blamed for upstream data problems as the top drivers — worth taking as a real signal about the complaint pattern, even if the exact percentage shouldn't be treated as gospel.
data-engineer.compStructure: Base + bonus + equity at most tech companies, overwhelmingly salaried rather than commission-based — this is a build/operate role, not a revenue-attributed one. Levels.fyi aggregate median total comp sits around $160K, but the spread by company is large: Capital One's median total comp is around $130K versus Netflix's roughly $565K, a gap driven almost entirely by equity rather than base. Consulting/managed-services shops (e.g., 3Cloud-style) skew toward base plus a smaller bonus, with less equity than product companies.
data-engineer.growthAreasTemplate: If {{growth_dimension}} showed up as a gap in your answers, it's worth digging into whether that reflects the actual job or just a version of it you haven't encountered yet. For example, if On-Call / Incident Appetite looked like a gap: this role carries a real operational load most people underestimate going in — formal on-call/pager rotations exist even at hyperscale (Uber runs dedicated on-call across 20,000+ pipelines), and being the default blame target when a number looks wrong, even when the root cause is upstream, is a documented and common frustration. If a lower appetite for that firefighting dynamic is a stable preference rather than inexperience, it's a legitimate reason to weigh this archetype against alternatives like analytics engineering, which sits closer to the SQL/transformation layer with less pipeline-ops exposure.
data-engineer.howToTestCheaply: Try building a small end-to-end pipeline yourself — pull data from a public API, land it in a warehouse (BigQuery/Snowflake free tier), add a basic freshness/schema-drift check, and let it run unattended for a week to see how you react when something breaks overnight. If you know a data engineer, ask to see their actual on-call runbook or their last postmortem — it'll tell you more about the real operational load than any job posting will.

developer-relations-advocacy.whatThisIs: Developer Relations / Developer Advocacy is public-facing technical content and community work — not marketing wearing an engineer's job title, whatever the reporting line says. The role spans four recurring buckets: technical content (blog posts, tutorials, sample apps, video), public speaking, community engagement (forums, meetups, Discord/Discord-adjacent moderation), and feeding developer friction back to product/engineering. It's explicitly not tied to accounts or tickets — success is measured by community and ecosystem health, not case resolution or account expansion — and DevRel practitioners are emphatic that hiring a marketer and calling them "Developer Advocate" gets seen through by developers immediately.
developer-relations-advocacy.whyMatchedTemplate:
You matched with Developer Relations / Developer Advocacy because your answers put {{top_dimension_1}}, {{top_dimension_2}}, and {{top_dimension_3}} close to what this role actually demands day to day — those are the parts of your profile pulling hardest toward this match.

For example: a profile with high **Public Visibility Comfort**, high **Ambiguity Tolerance**, and moderate **Travel / Physical Embed Willingness** would land here — someone whose primary job output is publicly-facing teaching content (tutorials, talks, videos) built for an audience who wasn't in the room, who's comfortable that no single trusted success metric exists for this work (active users, DQLs, and NPS all compete, and practitioners have had to invent proxy metrics for exactly that reason), and who's fine with conference travel being a real, recurring part of the job without needing it to dominate the calendar.
developer-relations-advocacy.aDayInThisRole: Your week mixes planned content production — drafting a tutorial, recording a demo video, writing a blog post based on real product usage — with community engagement: answering questions in a Discord or on Stack Overflow, moderating a forum, or running office hours that surface friction logs for product and engineering. Some weeks include travel for a conference talk or workshop; others are almost entirely remote content work, depending on how your specific role is weighted (a "Developer Experience Engineer" flavor skews toward SDKs/docs/CLI improvement with more code and less travel; a "Community" flavor skews toward events with less hands-on coding). You'll build sample apps and proofs-of-concept to demonstrate integration patterns, but that's in service of the content and community work, not the main deliverable. A recurring, real frustration: no one metric your organization trusts actually captures whether you're succeeding.
developer-relations-advocacy.compStructure: Base plus equity, with little to no sales-style variable comp — a structural difference from Sales/Solutions Engineering. Levels.fyi shows a median Developer Advocate salary around $156K across companies, rising sharply at big tech (Google $190K-$376K depending on level, median $217K; Amazon $165K-$236K, median $190K). The single biggest swing factor isn't company size — it's whether the role reports into engineering or marketing, which one compensation analysis says can set the pay band "before anything else." Discretionary annual bonuses exist; commission-style deal-linked pay does not.
developer-relations-advocacy.growthAreasTemplate: If {{growth_dimension}} came back lower than this role typically wants, that's worth a closer look rather than a red flag — it usually means a specific, testable gap, not a wrong match. For example: if your **Public Visibility Comfort** score is on the low side, know that this is the archetype's single defining trait — the job's primary, evaluated output is public one-to-many teaching content, not private peer communication or occasional documentation as a side effect of other work — if putting yourself out there publicly and repeatedly (not just occasionally explaining something to a teammate) doesn't sound appealing, that's the sharpest mismatch to weigh here, independent of your technical depth.
developer-relations-advocacy.howToTestCheaply: Give a lightning talk at a local meetup or write one public tutorial for a tool you actually use, then honestly ask yourself whether you'd want to do it again next month — the recurring, sustained cadence is what separates DevRel from an engineer who occasionally enjoys writing a blog post. Separately, spend a week answering questions in a public developer community (a product's Discord, a subreddit, Stack Overflow) and notice whether "translating for a stranger who wasn't in the room" energizes you or feels like a tax on real work — that's the job's daily texture, distilled.

embedded-iot-engineer.whatThisIs: Embedded/IoT Engineer covers writing and debugging firmware — mostly C/C++, occasionally Rust or Python for tooling — for microcontrollers, embedded Linux targets, and the hardware they run on, across consumer IoT, industrial, automotive, and medical-device contexts. It's not "knowing RTOS theory and interrupts cold" — practitioners describe it as a problem-solving discipline measured by live diagnostic speed: can you find why a $2M production line just crashed at 3 AM, not just explain memory models in the abstract. The field is also wider than "C on a microcontroller" implies — it spans bare-metal firmware, embedded Linux (sometimes in higher-level languages), wireless/connectivity work, and safety-critical avionics or medical firmware, each with very different stakes and pace.
embedded-iot-engineer.whyMatchedTemplate:
You matched with Embedded/IoT Engineer because your answers put {{top_dimension_1}}, {{top_dimension_2}}, and {{top_dimension_3}} close to what this role actually demands — those are the parts of your profile pulling hardest toward this match.

For example: a profile with high **Physical Constraint Engineering**, high **Debugging / Diagnostic Depth**, and moderate **Technical Breadth vs. Depth** would land here — someone who treats power, memory, and real-time budgets as pass/fail gates rather than soft targets, is comfortable diagnosing failures that could be hardware, firmware, or the interaction between the two, and is willing to build fluency in a second formal discipline (electrical engineering fundamentals) alongside software.
embedded-iot-engineer.aDayInThisRole: Expect hardware bring-up and validation work — getting firmware running on a new PCB revision, debugging with oscilloscopes, logic analyzers, or JTAG when something fails and it's not obvious yet whether the cause is hardware or code. You'll write and integrate drivers for communication protocols (UART, SPI, I²C, CAN, BLE, or cellular depending on the domain) and spend real time on resource-constrained optimization — power draw, memory footprint measured in kilobytes, and meeting real-time deadlines that don't bend. Testing and validation often follows formal compliance processes (medical, automotive, aerospace) most software engineers never encounter. A widely-cited rule of thumb in this field: the first 90% of a project is coding, the second 90% is debugging — schedule accordingly.
embedded-iot-engineer.compStructure: Comp is split more by industry segment than by seniority alone: big tech and tech-adjacent embedded roles (Google, Amazon, Nvidia) report medians in the $200K-$270K range, while non-VC-backed industrial, medical-device, automotive, and defense employers — where a large share of embedded roles actually live — run closer to $80K-$170K and are paid mostly in cash, with little or no equity. This is a structurally different comp mix than typical big-tech/startup software comp, and worth knowing going in rather than assuming embedded pay tracks general SWE pay.
embedded-iot-engineer.growthAreasTemplate: If {{growth_dimension}} came back lower than this role typically wants, treat it as a specific, checkable gap rather than a disqualifier. For example: if your **Physical Constraint Engineering** score is on the low side, know that this is close to the defining trait of the role — power/memory/timing budgets are absolute pass/fail constraints here, not CI-tracked soft targets like they might be in other engineering roles, and debugging at the hardware/software boundary (scope in hand) is routine rather than occasional.
embedded-iot-engineer.howToTestCheaply: Get a cheap dev board (Arduino, ESP32, or similar) and build something small end to end — sensor input, a wireless protocol, a real-time constraint you have to hit — to see whether hardware-software boundary debugging feels engaging or frustrating. Because self-taught entry here is genuinely harder than in mobile or web (there's no "ship an app to the store" equivalent low-cost proof point), also consider whether you can shadow or pair with an embedded engineer for a bring-up session before committing further — it'll surface the hardware-debugging temperament question faster than solo tinkering alone.

engineering-management.whatThisIs: Engineering Management is a track, not a promotion — and the honest version of it is "technical-adjacent," not "technical." The job is running one team's delivery, growth, and well-being: 1:1s, feedback, growth plans, hiring, and performance conversations are core, non-optional responsibilities, not add-ons to a technical job. Here's the part most engineers get wrong before they take the role: you stop being the primary implementer and become the primary evaluator of others' work. You stay "in the code" — reviewing PRs, weighing in on architecture — but the recommended hands-on-coding cadence is roughly a day every three to four months, and never in the critical path. If what you actually want is to keep shipping, this isn't that job with extra meetings; it's a different job.
engineering-management.whyMatchedTemplate:
You matched with Engineering Management because your answers put {{top_dimension_1}}, {{top_dimension_2}}, and {{top_dimension_3}} close to what this role actually demands day to day — those are the parts of your profile pulling hardest toward this match.

For example: a profile with high **People-Outcome Management Orientation**, moderate-to-high **Outcome Accountability**, and moderate-to-high **Interrupt Tolerance** would land here — someone who wants their own performance review to hinge on team retention, morale, and growth rather than personal output, is comfortable being on the hook for team-level delivery metrics they don't fully control hands-on, and doesn't mind moving between strategic planning and tactical firefighting (escalations, on-call handoffs) within the same hour.
engineering-management.aDayInThisRole: Expect 1:1s and continuous feedback conversations threaded through the day, plus a standing hiring pipeline (sourcing, interviewing, closing) that doesn't pause for a quiet sprint. You'll review pull requests and architectural proposals for signal, not to rewrite them, and coordinate with product and design on what's coming next. Escalations land on your desk — a stakeholder complaint, an on-call issue that needs a decision — and you'll move from a calm planning conversation into firefighting mode without much warning. A meaningful and often underestimated chunk of the week is emotional and organizational labor: absorbing team stress, managing up, and navigating conflict that has no ticket and no clean resolution.
engineering-management.compStructure: Read this part carefully if comp is part of why you're considering the move: EM is not a reliable path to more money. At Google, L6 Staff Engineer and L6 Engineering Manager total comp are effectively identical (~$580K–$591K median); at Amazon, L7 Principal SDE and L7 Senior SDM are likewise near-parity (~$651K–$654K). In AI/ML-heavy orgs the IC track can pull ahead outright — OpenAI's L6 IC median (~$1.24M) beats what management reaches at the same seniority. Structurally it's base + bonus + equity, the same shape as senior IC comp, not commission-based — and the first move into EM is typically a lateral move with no immediate bump. The level drives pay, not the track.
engineering-management.growthAreasTemplate: If {{growth_dimension}} came back lower than this role typically wants, that's worth a closer look rather than a red flag — it usually means a specific, testable gap, not a wrong match. For example: if your **People-Outcome Management Orientation** score is on the low side, know that this is the one dimension the whole archetype is built on — team retention, morale, and growth being the literal basis of your own performance review, not a side effect of doing good technical work. If that framing doesn't sound motivating, it's worth sitting with before you take the title, since it's the hardest part of the job to fake.
engineering-management.howToTestCheaply: Volunteer as tech lead on a project that requires giving another engineer direct, specific feedback on their work — not just reviewing their code, but coaching them through a gap — and notice whether that conversation energizes you or feels like a chore you're enduring to get back to "real work." Separately, run point on an escalation or cross-team coordination problem for a sprint and pay attention to whether the context-switching between planning and firefighting wears you down or feels like the interesting part of the job — that reaction is a more honest signal than whether you liked being a tech lead technically.

forward-deployed-engineer.whatThisIs: Forward Deployed Engineer (FDE, or Forward Deployed Software Engineer at Palantir specifically) means embedding directly with one customer to build and own the production system that customer actually runs — the inverse of a typical SWE's "one capability, many customers." The defining distinction from Solutions Architect: an SA designs the plan and hands it off for someone else to run; an FDE owns the customer outcome in production, and is evaluated as an engineer first — engineering reviews, code review, production monitoring — not on whether a sale closed. Don't mistake this for consulting with an engineering title: FDEs carry explicit product-development responsibility and ship code the client depends on, not one-off recommendations.
forward-deployed-engineer.whyMatchedTemplate:
You matched with Forward Deployed Engineer because your answers put {{top_dimension_1}}, {{top_dimension_2}}, and {{top_dimension_3}} close to what this role actually demands day to day — those are the parts of your profile pulling hardest toward this match.

For example: a profile with high **Outcome Accountability**, high **Coding Intensity**, and high **Relationship Continuity** would land here — someone who's genuinely comfortable being the named owner of a customer's production outcome even when upstream factors are outside their control, who wants coding to be the dominant activity of the week rather than an occasional prototype, and who'd rather go deep on one long-term embed than juggle a rotating portfolio of accounts.
forward-deployed-engineer.aDayInThisRole: Coding is the single biggest block of your week — architecting integration pipelines, mapping data schemas, building the dashboards or automated workflows the client actually depends on, all under the same engineering rigor (code review, deployability checks, production monitoring) as a core SWE role. But the week isn't uniform: some stretches look like conventional software engineering, others involve scoping a project's direction with the client directly, live-demoing to the client's own analysts, or pair-programming with the client's junior engineer so they can maintain the system after you roll off. You're embedded onsite a meaningful chunk of the time (Palantir's own figures run 25-50% onsite, up to 25% travel) and you're feeding field insight back into the core product roadmap.
forward-deployed-engineer.compStructure: This is the one archetype in the cluster with no quota, no commission, and no utilization target — comp is structured like core software engineering: base salary, a significant equity/RSU component, and an annual bonus, tied to deployment and delivery outcomes rather than sales attainment. Palantir FDSE runs $171K-$295K+ total comp (Levels.fyi, median ~$211K; NYC specifically $171K-$358K+), rising to $300K-$486K at senior and a $630K+ ceiling at staff/principal. Frontier AI labs pay a real premium concentrated in equity — OpenAI FDE roles are reported at $350K-$550K total comp for mid-to-senior levels — but the structure stays the same: this is not deal-linked variable pay like Sales Engineer or vendor-side SA, and it's not the utilization-linked bonus of consulting-side SA.
forward-deployed-engineer.growthAreasTemplate: If {{growth_dimension}} came back lower than this role typically wants, that's worth a closer look rather than a red flag — it usually means a specific, testable gap, not a wrong match. For example: if your **Outcome Accountability** score is on the low side, know that this is the sharpest anchor-5 case in the whole cluster — FDEs own the customer's production outcome even when contributing factors are out of their hands, in contrast to a Solutions Architect who hands off a design for someone else to run — and that's a real weight to sit with, not a formality, if you'd rather your accountability end at a recommendation or a design doc.
forward-deployed-engineer.howToTestCheaply: Volunteer for (or seek out) a customer-embed rotation or a deployment/onboarding engineering assignment at your current company, even a short one, and pay attention to whether owning a live system's outcome in front of the client who depends on it feels motivating or just stressful. Separately, try pairing with a non-engineer end user to help them use or extend something you built — the "leave the client able to maintain it themselves" pattern at the heart of this role is testable on a small scale without needing to switch jobs first.

ml-engineer.whatThisIs: An ML Engineer builds and ships production ML systems — training pipelines, feature engineering, model integration, deployment, and monitoring. The center of gravity is making ML work reliably in production, not inventing new algorithms or tuning novel architectures. The most common misconception going in: this is mostly deep learning and hyperparameter tuning. Reality, straight from practitioner accounts: ML-specific code is "just a few percents" of the codebase — the rest is data pipelines, APIs, monitoring, and production hardening, which is why one well-known practitioner calls the role "a full stack developer of the data science." It's also not research: companies explicitly prefer strong engineers who can pick up ML over ML theorists trying to pick up production engineering discipline.
ml-engineer.whyMatchedTemplate: Your answers point here because you scored high on {{top_dimension_1}}, {{top_dimension_2}}, and {{top_dimension_3}} — these track the traits that separate someone suited to production ML from someone drawn to research. A real profile landing here often scores high on Ambiguity Tolerance, Coding Intensity, and Outcome Accountability — you're comfortable translating a vague business problem into a measurable ML objective yourself, shipping production code is where most of your week actually goes, and you're motivated by A/B test outcomes and business KPIs moving, not by offline model metrics looking good in isolation.
ml-engineer.aDayInThisRole: Expect feature-engineering and data-pipeline work feeding both training and inference, training and evaluating models against a specific business metric (fraud scoring, churn prediction, personalization), and building the production system around the model — API integration, monitoring, CI/CD, and increasingly LLM fine-tuning or agentic-workflow integration even at companies that aren't AI-native. One practitioner's blunt summary: the job is "less about sophisticated transformer architectures and more about building resilient systems that can survive contact with reality," with most time spent getting a training set that faithfully represents the real-world distribution — once that's solved, a well-understood classical model is often good enough. At smaller companies, expect to build the platform and use it simultaneously, which one practitioner directly warns "is a quick way to burn out an individual or a team" if it isn't managed deliberately. Success is judged on shipped, reliable, business-impacting systems, not model sophistication or research novelty.
ml-engineer.compStructure: Comp mirrors general software engineering at the same company — this is typically a title/focus-tag on the generalist SWE ladder, not a separately-compensated track, so equity weighting grows sharply with seniority the same way it does for SWE (roughly 15-17% of total comp at entry level rising to 50-55% at senior/staff at Meta and Google). Cross-company aggregate median total comp is around $272,500, running meaningfully above general SWE and data-engineer bands at the same companies — a current market premium on ML talent, particularly at senior/staff levels. Entry-level real-world example: Affirm's MLE I posts $130K-$170K depending on location, notably lower than big-tech totals since it's base-only without a large RSU component.
ml-engineer.growthAreasTemplate: If {{growth_dimension}} showed up as a gap in your answers, it's worth checking whether that's a genuine mismatch or a skill you just haven't had reason to build yet. For example, if Ambiguity Tolerance looked like a gap: seniority in this role is explicitly measured by the ability to translate a vague business problem into a measurable ML objective yourself, without being handed a crisp spec or a single agreed success metric — junior MLE postings are comparatively rare precisely because this skill takes time to develop. If a lower score here reflects that you haven't yet had the chance to define a problem from scratch rather than a stable discomfort with ambiguity, that's a gap that closes with experience — but if open-ended problem framing itself feels aversive rather than just unfamiliar, that's worth taking seriously as a real signal.
ml-engineer.howToTestCheaply: Pick a public dataset and take a small model all the way to a deployed, monitored endpoint (not just a notebook with a good accuracy number) — the deployment, monitoring, and "what happens when the input distribution shifts" parts are the actual job, and a notebook-only project won't tell you anything about fit. Also read Chip Huyen's ML Interviews Book chapter comparing MLE to SWE and to data scientist — it's a fast, direct way to see whether the actual center of gravity (engineering discipline over algorithmic novelty) matches what drew you to ML in the first place.

mobile-engineer.whatThisIs: Mobile Engineer covers building and shipping the native (or cross-platform) app itself — iOS, Android, or both, via Swift/SwiftUI, Kotlin/Jetpack Compose, or a shared framework like React Native or Flutter. It's easy to file this under "UI work" and move on, but the job spans real systems-engineering problems: offline-first data sync, memory- and battery-constrained performance tuning, binary size budgets, and a release process with no instant rollback once a build ships to the store. The underlying skill — structuring an app, managing state, working with APIs — transfers across iOS, Android, and even backend work more readily than the "iOS developer" or "Android developer" label suggests.
mobile-engineer.whyMatchedTemplate:
You matched with Mobile Engineer because your answers put {{top_dimension_1}}, {{top_dimension_2}}, and {{top_dimension_3}} close to what this role actually demands day to day — those are the parts of your profile pulling hardest toward this match.

For example: a profile with high **Coding Intensity**, moderate **Physical Constraint Engineering**, and moderate **Debugging / Diagnostic Depth** would land here — someone who wants to be hands-on-keyboard building the shipped product most of the week, is comfortable treating performance budgets (cold start, battery, frame drops) as real constraints, and can debug across the app-code/OS boundary without it being the defining feature of the job.
mobile-engineer.aDayInThisRole: Most of the day is building UI and business logic against a design spec, then wiring it to backend APIs and handling the parts of that connection a backend engineer never has to think about — flaky networks, offline caching, sync conflicts. You'll profile startup time, memory footprint, or jank on a mid-tier Android device because those numbers are gated in CI, not just nice-to-haves. Release weeks bring their own rhythm: App Store or Play Store submission, staged rollouts, and watching crash-reporting dashboards (Crashlytics/Sentry) since you can't hotfix a shipped binary the way you'd redeploy a server. At senior+, expect code review, mentoring, and — eventually — owning modularization or build-time decisions that affect the whole team, not just your feature.
mobile-engineer.compStructure: Base + equity (RSUs at public/late-stage companies, options earlier) + bonus — the same general shape as other software engineering roles, with no separate variable or commission component. Reported medians cluster around $190K-$220K total comp depending on platform and source, with big tech (Meta, LinkedIn) running meaningfully higher and smaller/non-tech-hub employers running lower — company tier moves the number more than iOS vs. Android does.
mobile-engineer.growthAreasTemplate: If {{growth_dimension}} came back lower than this role typically wants, that's worth a closer look rather than a red flag — it usually means a specific, testable gap, not a wrong match. For example: if your **Systems Design at Scale** score is on the low side, know that this role is mostly scoped to one app or team rather than a platform serving many consuming teams — real architectural ownership shows up at staff+ but stays bounded to your own codebase, which is worth weighing if you're specifically looking for platform-scale design work.
mobile-engineer.howToTestCheaply: Build and ship something small to the App Store or Play Store yourself — even a single-screen utility app — and see whether the release-and-rollback constraints (no instant hotfix, review queue delays) feel like a real constraint or an annoyance. Separately, spend a week profiling an existing open-source app's cold-start time or memory footprint with the platform's own tooling (Xcode Instruments, Android Studio Profiler) to get a direct read on whether that kind of performance-budget work is engaging or tedious to you.

platform-infrastructure-engineer.whatThisIs: A Platform / Infrastructure Engineer builds and operates the internal developer platform — self-service infra, golden paths, CI/CD, provisioning APIs — that lets product teams ship without needing deep infra expertise themselves. The key distinction from "rebranded DevOps": DevOps solves one team's pipeline problem, platform engineering solves the same class of problem at organizational scale, as a reusable product other engineers adopt. It's easy to mistake this for a pure ops/infra-code job — it isn't. Multiple sources are blunt that this is as much a product-mindset job as a technical one, and engineers who just want to write infra code in isolation, with no requirements-gathering or internal advocacy, tend to be a poor fit despite the technical overlap.
platform-infrastructure-engineer.whyMatchedTemplate: Your answers point here because you scored high on {{top_dimension_1}}, {{top_dimension_2}}, and {{top_dimension_3}} — these are the traits that separate someone who thrives building shared infrastructure from someone who'd rather build one team's feature. A real profile landing here often scores high on Systems Design at Scale, Stakeholder & Client-Facing Comfort, and Ambiguity Tolerance — you want to design things meant to hold up across many consuming teams, you're energized (not drained) by gathering requirements from other engineers as if they were customers, and you're comfortable defining a "minimum viable platform" yourself rather than being handed a spec.
platform-infrastructure-engineer.aDayInThisRole: You'll manage cloud infrastructure and IaC (Terraform, Pulumi), operate Kubernetes clusters, and write client libraries other engineers pull directly into their own services — real production code, not just config. A meaningful chunk of your week goes to requirements-gathering: meeting with internal "customer" teams, defining what the platform should do next, and iterating based on their feedback rather than executing a top-down mandate. At data-heavy orgs this specializes into data-platform work; at smaller companies (sub-500 engineers) this role frequently blends with SRE work, so read the actual responsibilities section of any posting, not just the title. Success is measured by adoption and whether product engineers are shipping faster with less infra knowledge required — a technically elegant platform nobody uses is a failure by this role's own standard.
platform-infrastructure-engineer.compStructure: Base-heavy with equity, mirroring general software engineering comp — there's no material sales-style variable comp here. Reported 2025 US median base is roughly $172K with median total comp around $235K at mid-career, rising past $400K total at Staff level; Bay Area runs higher (median base ~$198K, total ~$310K). AI-infrastructure-adjacent platform roles currently command a premium, roughly $180K-$310K at mid-to-senior levels. There's no separate "platform engineer" comp track on levels.fyi distinct from general senior SWE bands — it's priced as a software engineering specialization, not its own discipline.
platform-infrastructure-engineer.growthAreasTemplate: If {{growth_dimension}} showed up as a gap in your answers, it's worth pausing on this one specifically rather than assuming it's just unfamiliarity. For example, if Stakeholder & Client-Facing Comfort looked like a gap: this isn't a peripheral skill here, it's the single highest-weighted trait in the role. "Platform as a product" means your daily counterparts are other engineering teams whose adoption you have to earn, not a spec you're handed — practitioners explicitly warn that engineers who dislike this kind of internal advocacy struggle here even when their technical infra skills are strong. If this gap is real for you, it's worth weighing seriously rather than assuming it'll fade with practice.
platform-infrastructure-engineer.howToTestCheaply: Find a "platform engineering" or "developer platform" team at a company you know and ask to sit in on one of their internal roadmap or requirements-gathering meetings with a consuming team — that conversation is the actual job in miniature. Alternatively, try building a small internal tool (a shared CI template, a self-service provisioning script) for a team you're not on and see whether the requirements-gathering and adoption-chasing part of it energizes you or feels like a tax on the "real" engineering work.

product-full-stack-software-engineer.whatThisIs: A Product / Full-Stack Software Engineer takes a product requirement from design/PM handoff through implementation, code review, and deploy — moving across frontend, backend, or both depending on team need and company stage. The title covers "React dev," "backend Go person," and "generalist" alike: real postings level and pay these identically, with stack emphasis treated as a skill lean within one role, not a separate job. The part most people get wrong going in: writing new code is roughly 15-20% of the job — the rest is review, debugging, planning, and cross-functional back-and-forth with product and design.
product-full-stack-software-engineer.whyMatchedTemplate: Your answers point here because you scored high on {{top_dimension_1}}, {{top_dimension_2}}, and {{top_dimension_3}} — all central to how day-to-day work actually breaks down in this role. For example, a real profile that lands here often scores high on Coding Intensity, Technical Breadth vs. Depth, and Ambiguity Tolerance — you want shipped code to be the actual deliverable, you're comfortable moving across frontend and backend as the team needs, and you can work from a rough brief without everything being pre-specified by a ticket.
product-full-stack-software-engineer.aDayInThisRole: Expect feature work that starts from a PM/design handoff and ends with something running in production — architecting a piece end-to-end, not just implementing a spec. You'll spend real time in code review and, as you get senior, mentoring — the job functionally shifts from "ship code" to "make the people around you better." Cross-functional syncs with product, design, and adjacent engineering teams are a constant, not occasional. If your team owns its own services, expect on-call in a fair rotation, more common at Series B+ companies than pre-seed. By 2026, AI-assisted development (LLM/RAG integration into product features) shows up as a baseline expectation in postings, not a differentiator.
product-full-stack-software-engineer.compStructure: Base-heavy at small/mid companies, shifting to base+equity at scale-ups and base+bonus+heavy-RSU at big tech — there's no variable/commission component here at any stage. Aggregate market median total comp is around $192K (25th percentile ~$135K, 75th ~$277K), with new grads around $140K; at big tech, base salary barely moves between senior and staff, so the real differentiator at that level is equity and bonus, not base. A representative mid-size example: WHOOP posts Senior Software Engineer (Full Stack) base at $150K-$210K.
product-full-stack-software-engineer.growthAreasTemplate: If {{growth_dimension}} showed up as a gap in your answers, it's worth checking whether that's a real mismatch with this role or just something you haven't been tested on yet. For example, if Systems Design at Scale looked like a gap: most product engineers operate at the level of a single feature or service for one product, not platform-scale systems serving many teams — that only becomes a real demand at staff+ level, where technical direction spans multiple teams. If you're earlier-career or targeting a smaller company, a lower score here often just reflects that you haven't been asked to operate at that scope yet, not that you're a poor fit for the role as it exists day-to-day.
product-full-stack-software-engineer.howToTestCheaply: Pull up 3-4 real postings across company stages (an early-stage startup, a scale-up like WHOOP, and a big-tech listing) and read the actual bullet points, not just the title — see how much of your week would go to feature ownership versus meetings/review at each stage. If you can, shadow a code review session or a sprint planning meeting at a company your size to get a feel for how much of the job is collaboration versus heads-down building before you commit to the switch.

sales-engineer-pre-sales.whatThisIs: Sales Engineer (also called Solutions Engineer at many SaaS companies — same job, different label; not the vendor-side Solutions Architect or Customer Solutions Engineer roles that share that name) is the technical half of a two-person deal team. The Account Executive owns the relationship and commercial terms; you own proving the product actually works for this prospect's real, often-messy environment. That means live discovery, tailored demos, and running Proof-of-Concept engagements where the "technical win" gets earned or lost — not reading from a canned deck. The bar to stay credible in front of a skeptical staff engineer on the other side of the table is genuinely high; you can't fake your way through a live technical objection.
sales-engineer-pre-sales.whyMatchedTemplate:
You matched with Sales Engineer / Pre-Sales Engineer because your answers put {{top_dimension_1}}, {{top_dimension_2}}, and {{top_dimension_3}} close to what this role actually demands day to day — those are the parts of your profile pulling hardest toward this match.

For example: a profile with high **Variable Compensation Appetite**, high **Stakeholder & Client-Facing Comfort**, and moderate **Outcome Accountability** would land here — someone who wants real upside/downside tied to deal outcomes rather than a flat salary, who treats shifting register between a skeptical engineer and a business buyer as an enjoyable daily mode rather than a tax, and who's comfortable owning the "technical win" of a deal without needing to control what happens to the product after the contract is signed.
sales-engineer-pre-sales.aDayInThisRole: Your day is built around a handful of scheduled discovery calls, demos, and POC check-ins rather than a queue or a page. You're running discovery to understand a prospect's stack and buying criteria, then customizing a demo to that environment instead of running the same script twice. When a POC is live, you're standing up trial environments, debugging live failures with the deal and the AE watching, and writing RFP responses technical reviewers on the prospect side will actually scrutinize. You're also feeding structured win/loss and competitive-positioning feedback back to product — a channel most engineers never get.
sales-engineer-pre-sales.compStructure: This is the defining feature of the archetype: SE comp runs 70/30 or 80/20 base-to-variable, with 80/20 most common at SaaS companies — a $175K OTE SE is roughly $140K base plus $35K at-quota variable. That variable is tied to your aligned AE's or territory's attainment, not an individual quota, so your income has real quarter-to-quarter swing tied to deals you influence but don't fully control. Levels.fyi puts median US base around $147K and median OTE around $205K, with top performers at well-known SaaS vendors clearing $320K+ OTE — don't mistake this for "engineer salary with a small bonus."
sales-engineer-pre-sales.growthAreasTemplate: If {{growth_dimension}} came back lower than this role typically wants, that's worth a closer look rather than a red flag — it usually means a specific, testable gap, not a wrong match. For example: if your **Variable Compensation Appetite** score is on the low side, know that this is the single sharpest structural difference between this role and almost every other engineering archetype — 20-30% of your OTE moves with deals you're not the only one influencing, and that volatility is a real trade, not a technicality, worth sitting with before you commit to it.
sales-engineer-pre-sales.howToTestCheaply: Shadow an SE on a live discovery call or demo at your current company (or ask your network for an intro) and watch how they handle an unscripted technical objection in real time — that improvisational, audience-pressure debugging is the actual job, not the slide deck around it. Separately, volunteer to demo your own team's product or feature to a non-technical stakeholder or a prospective customer once; if narrating and defending your work live to someone who can say "no" feels energizing rather than draining, that's a real signal.

security-engineer.whatThisIs: Security Engineer covers designing security architecture, running risk and vulnerability assessments, and — increasingly — partnering directly with product and engineering teams to build security into the software development lifecycle rather than gatekeeping it at the end. The umbrella splits into real sub-specializations (cloud security, application security, network/infrastructure security, detection & response) that hire somewhat differently, but they share a common core. The "security cop who says no to everything" stereotype is largely wrong for how the job is actually described in postings: the highest-leverage work is collaborative — consulting with developers, educating teams on secure coding, and being judged on whether people actually adopt the guardrails you build, not just whether the guardrails exist. It's also not primarily offensive hacking; most of the profession is defense, incident response, risk management, and architecture.
security-engineer.whyMatchedTemplate:
You matched with Security Engineer because your answers put {{top_dimension_1}}, {{top_dimension_2}}, and {{top_dimension_3}} close to what this role actually demands — those are the parts of your profile pulling hardest toward this match.

For example: a profile with high **Adversarial / Threat-Model Thinking**, high **Ambiguity Tolerance**, and moderate-high **Stakeholder & Client-Facing Comfort** would land here — someone whose default mental model for any system is "how would someone try to break this," who's comfortable operating with minimal specs on problems that don't fit existing frameworks, and who treats consulting with and educating other engineering teams as core work rather than a distraction from "real" security work.
security-engineer.aDayInThisRole: Expect a mix of hands-on and advisory work: reviewing architecture for a new service with an eye toward exploitable weaknesses, triaging and tracking remediation of vulnerability findings, and consulting with a product team on secure-by-default patterns before code ships rather than after. Detection-and-response-leaning roles spend more time building and tuning detection systems and owning live incident response when something does go wrong — parallel to SRE's incident work but scoped to security events specifically. Application-security-leaning roles look more like embedded partnership: pairing with developers, writing rules for tools like Semgrep, and being judged on developer adoption of what you build. Staying current on the threat landscape and translating compliance regimes (SOC 2, PCI DSS, NIST) into concrete engineering asks is a recurring undercurrent regardless of specialization.
security-engineer.compStructure: Base-heavy with equity, tracking general software engineering comp discipline rather than a separate security pay scale. Reported medians run roughly $240K-$300K at large tech companies (Google, Amazon, Nvidia, Cisco), with a real posted Staff+ range at a frontier AI lab of $320K-$485K. Entry-level security work (SOC analyst and similar feeder roles, not yet titled "security engineer") pays far less — commonly $70K-$85K — and the realistic path to "security engineer" titling and the higher bands above typically takes 5-8 years, not a fast bootcamp-to-six-figures jump.
security-engineer.growthAreasTemplate: If {{growth_dimension}} came back lower than this role typically wants, that's a specific, checkable gap rather than a sign you're in the wrong place. For example: if your **Adversarial / Threat-Model Thinking** score is on the low side, that's worth attention here specifically — it's the single highest-weighted trait for this archetype, since defaulting to "how would someone exploit this" rather than "what's the most likely accidental failure" is close to the defining lens of the job, not an optional add-on skill.
security-engineer.howToTestCheaply: Pick a system you already know well (a side project, or a service at work you have access to) and run a real threat-modeling pass on it — write down concrete attack paths, not just a generic checklist — and notice whether that exercise energizes you or feels like a chore. Separately, most security-engineering paths run through a SOC-analyst or application-security on-ramp; shadowing a SOC analyst's shift or pairing with an AppSec engineer on a code review for a day is a low-cost way to see the entry-level reality (queue-driven triage, developer consultations) before committing to the multi-year path toward the senior comp figures above.

solutions-architect-consulting.whatThisIs: Solutions Architect (Consulting-Side) is the systems-integrator/consultancy version of this title — Accenture, Deloitte, Slalom, Capgemini, and similar — where you design across many vendors' products for a given client engagement rather than going deep on one vendor's stack. That's the real dividing line from the vendor-side version of this same title: a consulting SA is paid to recommend the best solution across the client's mixed, changing environment, even when that means recommending against your own firm's practice offering. You typically split time across pre-sale/proposal work and delivery oversight for a portfolio of concurrent client engagements, not one long embed — and while the role does carry real project-management weight, postings (Thoughtworks among them) explicitly expect you to keep coding alongside developers, not just produce diagrams.
solutions-architect-consulting.whyMatchedTemplate:
You matched with Solutions Architect (Consulting-Side) because your answers put {{top_dimension_1}}, {{top_dimension_2}}, and {{top_dimension_3}} close to what this role actually demands day to day — those are the parts of your profile pulling hardest toward this match.

For example: a profile with high **Technical Breadth vs. Depth**, low **Variable Compensation Appetite**, and high **Stakeholder & Client-Facing Comfort** would land here — someone who wants to range across platforms, integration technologies, and cloud stacks rather than specialize in one vendor, who's comfortable with comp that's utilization-linked rather than commission-linked, and who's energized leading workshops and steering committees across a client's technical and business stakeholders.
solutions-architect-consulting.aDayInThisRole: Your day centers on solutioning workshops, architectural reviews, and discovery sessions that translate a client's often-competing priorities (cost, flexibility, service quality) into a concrete value proposition and solution blueprint. You'll produce sequence diagrams, integration models, and level-of-effort estimates that feed an RFP or a signed SOW, and act as the alignment point between the bid team and the client through both proposal and delivery phases. Expect real cross-vendor/cross-platform integration work — provisioning environments, governing clusters, evaluating security and cost tradeoffs across multiple cloud stacks — plus the ongoing reality of being staffed across more than one client engagement at a time, with utilization tracked closely as your core performance number.
solutions-architect-consulting.compStructure: Per the resolved comp figures for this archetype (Levels.fyi as the primary, level-segmented anchor): Accenture Solution Architects run from Associate at $115K up to Team Leader at $169K, with Senior Solution Architect median total comp around $196K (base ~$181K + modest stock + roughly $11.5K bonus — about 6% of base). This is base-plus-modest-bonus, not commission: the bonus ties to utilization and project margin, not deal-by-deal attainment, and equity is essentially absent below partner/MD level. Treat wider Glassdoor-aggregate ranges (which blend many levels and geographies into one number and run meaningfully higher at the top end) as the overall observed market spread, not a conflicting figure — the Levels.fyi numbers above are the better per-level anchor. Whichever number you're looking at, the structural point holds: this is a utilization-linked bonus, not the deal-linked variable comp of Sales Engineer or vendor-side SA, and not the equity-heavy, no-quota comp of Forward Deployed Engineer.
solutions-architect-consulting.growthAreasTemplate: If {{growth_dimension}} came back lower than this role typically wants, that's worth a closer look rather than a red flag — it usually means a specific, testable gap, not a wrong match. For example: if your **Technical Breadth vs. Depth** score is on the low side, know that this role is explicitly contrasted against vendor-side SA on this exact axis — consulting SAs need working fluency across many platforms and enterprise patterns rather than deep specialization in one, and a preference for going deep in a single stack is worth weighing seriously against a role built around a client's mixed, changing environment.
solutions-architect-consulting.howToTestCheaply: Take on (or volunteer for) a cross-team or cross-vendor integration problem at your current job — something that forces you to reason across two unfamiliar platforms or cloud stacks rather than your usual one — and notice whether the context-switching feels energizing or just tiring. Separately, try drafting a solution blueprint or level-of-effort estimate for a hypothetical multi-vendor client problem and see whether you'd rather own that design document or be the one implementing it — that distinction (design-and-handoff versus build-and-run) is the sharpest real test of fit for this archetype.

solutions-architect-vendor-side.whatThisIs: Solutions Architect (Vendor-Side) means you're employed by the product company (AWS, Salesforce, GCP, and similar) and your job is making that company's product look — and actually be — right for a prospect or client, across bigger and more strategic accounts than a typical Sales Engineer usually touches. This isn't "SE with a fancier title": you own multi-year transformation narratives, executive relationships, and future-state architecture, not single-product demos. Note the naming trap — some vendors use "Solutions Engineer" as a synonym for this role, others use it for Sales Engineer, and it's a different thing again from a post-sale Customer Solutions Engineer. Title alone won't tell you which one a posting means; scope and account size will.
solutions-architect-vendor-side.whyMatchedTemplate:
You matched with Solutions Architect (Vendor-Side) because your answers put {{top_dimension_1}}, {{top_dimension_2}}, and {{top_dimension_3}} close to what this role actually demands day to day — those are the parts of your profile pulling hardest toward this match.

For example: a profile with high **Stakeholder & Client-Facing Comfort**, high **Variable Compensation Appetite**, and moderate **Outcome Accountability** would land here — someone who's energized running consultations across engineers, procurement, and C-suite sponsors in the same week, who's comfortable with a real (if more base-weighted than an SE's) slice of comp tied to bookings and win rate, and who's fine owning a deal's technical outcome without owning what happens to the deployed system long after the ink dries.
solutions-architect-vendor-side.aDayInThisRole: Expect discovery workshops and value-stream assessments that define scope collaboratively with the client rather than starting from a fixed spec. You'll lead Proof-of-Concept or Proof-of-Value work directly against a prospect's real data and infrastructure — hands-on, not slideware — and produce the architecture diagrams, TCO analyses, and migration plans that back a large deal. At AWS-class vendors this role is explicitly described as "the technical glue" between support, service, and sales teams, which means real cross-team coordination alongside client meetings, plus periodic travel for onsite sessions and migration work.
solutions-architect-vendor-side.compStructure: Vendor-side SA comp includes sales-KPI-linked variable pay — bookings, expansion, adoption, win rate are named success metrics — but the split is usually more base-weighted than a pure SE's, not commission-driven in the same aggressive way. At public cloud vendors, senior SAs typically run $165K-$210K base plus roughly 15% target bonus plus RSUs, for total comp in the $250K-$340K range; Levels.fyi shows Amazon Solutions Architect spanning $167K (L4) to $432K+ (L7) with median total comp around $279K, and Salesforce Solution Architect running $150K (Associate) to $284K+ (Principal). The through-line: real attainment-linked variable exposure, but softer than SE, and no formal utilization target the way consulting-side SA has.
solutions-architect-vendor-side.growthAreasTemplate: If {{growth_dimension}} came back lower than this role typically wants, that's worth a closer look rather than a red flag — it usually means a specific, testable gap, not a wrong match. For example: if your **Stakeholder & Client-Facing Comfort** score is on the low side, know that this role's identity is built around being a trusted advisor who shifts register fluidly across a skeptical engineer, a procurement stakeholder, and an executive sponsor — often within the same week — so a lower score here is worth taking seriously before assuming the technical parts of the job will be enough to carry you through it.
solutions-architect-vendor-side.howToTestCheaply: Ask to sit in on (or help prep for) a discovery workshop or executive-level solution review at your current company, even informally, and notice whether translating technical tradeoffs for a non-technical sponsor feels like a natural extension of your engineering thinking or like a separate, draining skill. Separately, try building a TCO or migration-plan document for a real (even hypothetical) customer scenario using a vendor's public architecture framework — the artifact-production side of this role is testable without needing the job title first.

sre-production-engineer.whatThisIs: An SRE / Production Engineer owns service reliability: SLOs and error budgets, incident response, on-call, and the resilience patterns (retries, circuit breakers, graceful degradation) that keep other teams' services up. This isn't sysadmin work with a rebrand — Google's own hiring bar requires years of coding and automation experience (Python, distributed systems), and comp bands track software engineering ladders, not IT/ops ladders. It's also not "systems work in the abstract" — this role specifically rewards people who enjoy incident composure under real time pressure. If incident response itself doesn't appeal to you, platform engineering is probably the better fit even though the tooling looks nearly identical.
sre-production-engineer.whyMatchedTemplate: Your answers point here because you scored high on {{top_dimension_1}}, {{top_dimension_2}}, and {{top_dimension_3}} — these track closely with what actually distinguishes SRE from adjacent infra roles. A real profile landing here often scores high on On-Call / Incident Appetite, Debugging / Diagnostic Depth, and Outcome Accountability — you're comfortable owning live, high-stakes incidents in real time, you can diagnose an unfamiliar production issue across multiple failure domains under a clock, and you're willing to be the named owner of an SLO even when the underlying code is someone else's.
sre-production-engineer.aDayInThisRole: Google's own doctrine caps operational/reactive work at half your time, reserving the rest for project work — building monitoring and alerting tooling, defining SLOs, and driving resilience patterns across services other teams own. In practice that split slips: the 2025 SRE Report found a median of 30% operational time, up from 25% the year before, so ask specifically about the on-call load and project/ops ratio in any interview rather than assuming either extreme. You'll carry on-call rotations that can run 24/7 follow-the-sun across regions, own escalation during live outages, and drive postmortems afterward. You're also writing real production code (commonly Python or Go) and influencing architecture and standards for the services you support — this is an engineering discipline, not a ticket queue.
sre-production-engineer.compStructure: Base-heavy with equity, tracking standard software engineering ladders at the same company rather than an IT/ops pay scale. On-call is generally not paid as separate variable comp at most big tech companies — it's folded into base/equity/bonus — though some companies do pay explicit stipends, and this varies enough that it's worth asking directly rather than assuming either way. levels.fyi-reported total comp for the title runs from roughly $197K (Google L3) up to $768K (Google L7), with an overall reported median around $203,600 across companies.
sre-production-engineer.growthAreasTemplate: If {{growth_dimension}} showed up as a gap in your answers, it's worth being honest with yourself about whether that's a real mismatch here, since this is one dimension where the role doesn't bend much. For example, if On-Call / Incident Appetite looked like a gap: this is the defining trait of the role, not a peripheral requirement — the job explicitly rewards people who find live-outage pressure and problem-solving energizing rather than distressing, and practitioner accounts describe burnout and exit as common enough to have a visible literature when that appetite isn't genuinely there. If this gap is real, it's a legitimate signal to look at platform engineering instead, which shares most of the same tooling without the incident-response core.
sre-production-engineer.howToTestCheaply: Ask an SRE at a company you're interested in if you can shadow one on-call shift (even passively, watching how they triage an alert) to see how it actually feels under a live page. Separately, try running a small "game day" for yourself — deliberately break something in a side project or staging environment and time how you handle the diagnosis and fix under self-imposed pressure — it's a decent proxy for whether incident composure is something you enjoy or just tolerate.

technical-product-manager.whatThisIs: Technical Product Manager, scoped here to PMs owning genuinely technical surfaces — APIs, SDKs, developer platforms, internal tooling, ML infra — where reading code and specs is a daily requirement, not a resume line. Say this plainly up front: you won't keep coding. The job is design reviews, spec discussions, and roadmap ownership for a technical surface; your technical fluency gets used to evaluate and shape engineering work — judging estimates, arguing architecture trade-offs, deciding build-vs-buy — not to build it yourself. A lot of engineers walk in expecting the technical parts to transfer directly and are caught off guard by how amorphous the output feels: the meeting is the work, not a means to a commit.
technical-product-manager.whyMatchedTemplate:
You matched with Technical Product Manager because your answers put {{top_dimension_1}}, {{top_dimension_2}}, and {{top_dimension_3}} close to what this role actually demands day to day — those are the parts of your profile pulling hardest toward this match.

For example: a profile with high **Ambiguity Tolerance**, moderate-to-high **Stakeholder & Client-Facing Comfort**, and moderate **Systems Design at Scale** would land here — someone who's energized by owning a roadmap with no single agreed success metric and making prioritization calls under real ambiguity, is comfortable being judged on cross-functional stakeholder satisfaction rather than a personal deliverable, and can reason about architecture trade-offs (build-vs-buy, API lifecycle decisions) well enough to shape them without personally implementing them.
technical-product-manager.aDayInThisRole: Most of the day is conversations with engineers and the writing that follows them — a design review that quietly decides whether a feature ships this sprint or gets parked for a quarter. You're owning roadmap and requirements for a technical surface engineers themselves consume: defining API specs, data models, auth strategy, deprecation timelines. You're making the calls engineers feel directly — build vs. buy, whether a "small" ask is actually a six-week effort with on-call risk baked in — and coordinating with engineering, data science, UX, and client success to sequence delivery. Reading code and specs closely enough to catch a bad estimate is routine; writing production code is not.
technical-product-manager.compStructure: TPM carries a real, documented premium over general PM comp: levels.fyi puts median Technical Product Manager total comp at $251,000 against $228,750 for general PM — the market pricing in the scarcity of technical fluency layered onto product skills. Structure is base + bonus + equity (RSU-heavy at public companies, options at startups), similar in shape to senior IC engineering comp, not commission-heavy. Range varies enormously by company — Nvidia spans roughly $123K–$656K+, Google spans $198K–$2.45M+ across levels — so company choice matters as much as the TPM-vs-PM distinction itself. Treat levels.fyi figures as the ceiling estimate for well-funded tech companies; Glassdoor's broader sample skews lower and is more representative of the wider market.
technical-product-manager.growthAreasTemplate: If {{growth_dimension}} came back lower than this role typically wants, that's worth a closer look rather than a red flag — it usually means a specific, testable gap, not a wrong match. For example: if your **Ambiguity Tolerance** score is on the low side, know that this archetype is built around owning a roadmap with no crisp, agreed success metric — deciding what "success" even means as part of the job, not just executing against one someone else defined. If you want the goal to crystallize before you start working, that's a real friction point here, not a minor adjustment.
technical-product-manager.howToTestCheaply: Volunteer to run a design review or spec discussion for a feature you won't personally implement — make the build-vs-buy or scope call, then hand it off and let someone else own the code — and see whether shaping the decision feels satisfying on its own or like an unfinished task because you didn't get to write it. Separately, spend a sprint owning cross-functional coordination for one technical initiative (engineering, design, data) with a contested or fuzzy success metric, and notice whether the ambiguity of "what does done even mean here" feels like the interesting part of the job or a source of low-grade anxiety you're tolerating.
